description: T.M.H. Rifas Vs Director General of Customs and Others before Hon. Dr. D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
parties: T.M.H. Rifas Vs Director General of Customs and Others
judgement_by: Hon. Dr. D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
keywords: The Petitioner, being the importer on record registered under Section 115 of the Customs Ordinance, had imported certain consignment of goods, part of which was not declared in the Customs Declaration form. It was investigated into since there was a mismatch between the Customs Declaration and the Bill of Lading and the packing list. After inquiry, the goods were ordered to be released on payment of Custom duty and certain penalty. Subsequently, the producing officer made an application to the Director General to revise the order and sought a direction under Section 2 read with Section 47 as well. After full-scale inquiry, penalty was imposed under Section 47; Petitioner challenges the same. Held – the Respondents’ actions are legal and justifiable, and the order made under Section 47 is justifiable as well.
legistation: The Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka

judgement_by: Hon. Dr. D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
keywords: The Petitioner, a Class II officer, responded to an advertisement to be promoted as a Class I officer before the given deadline. However, the deadline was extended, and the applicants were given a grace period to obtain the necessary results of the Efficiency Bar exam and language proficiency exam to meet the criteria required to apply for the said promotion. The Petitioner was unsuccessful in securing the appointment; challenged the said extensions and new appointments. Held – extensions were granted at the request of all the members of the cadre; therefore, no prejudice was caused since a more competitive level playing field was created.
legistation: The Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka

judgement_by: Hon. Dr. D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
keywords: The Petitioner had filed an application to obtain a Faskh divorce before the Quazi Court of Gampaha. However, later having withdrawn that application, she had changed her residence to Panadura, where she had instituted a similar application. The Respondent had raised objections to the maintainability because she had recently come to reside within the jurisdiction of the Panadura Quazi Court; objection overruled, and proceeded with the divorce. Later, the Respondent, without notice, obtained a stay order from the Board of Quazi, and thereafter notified the Petitioner. Since there is a procedural error made by the Board of Quazi by not hearing the Petitioner, Court granted relief as prayed for
legistation: Muslim Marriage and Divorce Act
